context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Multi-level Planning and Scale

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of power and legitimacy in multi-level planning? Is the State a site or an actor in multi-level planning? What is the role of resources in multi-level planning? How do collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning efforts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on multi-level planning, and I’m kinda stuck on the power/legitimacy part rn. Can u help me figure out how to answer the prompt without making it too vague?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we dig in — when you think about multi-level planning (local, national, global levels), who do you think gets to decide what gets planned, and why would other actors accept those decisions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help on the power/legitimacy part of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding too much upfront, I should ask a probing Socratic question to get them thinking about what they already know. This will reveal their baseline understanding and let me guide from there. I'll keep it bite-sized and open-ended.

exchanges.student: I think mostly the state decides, but NGOs, donors, and local groups matter too. I’m just not fully sure if ppl accept decisions bc of legal authority, resources, or like actual trust/legitimacy.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — you've actually identified three distinct things there. Do you think legal authority, resources, and trust/legitimacy are separate sources of power, or do they depend on each other in some way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuinely interesting tension between three sources of compliance: legal authority, resources, and legitimacy/trust. This is actually a productive distinction that maps well onto the prompt's questions about power and legitimacy. Rather than explaining the distinction for them, I should push them to think about whether these three things are separate or related, which will help them develop their own argument.

exchanges.student: I think they’re separate but also kinda overlap tbh. Like a state can have legal authority, but if it has no resources or public trust, its planning power seems weak.
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking. Now take that one step further — if power is spread unevenly across levels (say, a donor holds resources, a national state holds legal authority, but local communities hold trust), what does that mean for who actually controls planning outcomes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress, arriving organically at the idea that legitimacy is multi-dimensional and that these sources of power are interdependent. This is solid thinking that connects well to the prompt. Rather than validating and moving on, I should push them to apply this insight specifically to the multi-level context - what happens when these sources of power are distributed across different levels?

exchanges.student: It seems like control gets split, not fully owned by one actor. But idk how to say that clearly in the paper without sounding messy.
exchanges.tutor: Writing clarity will come from thinking clarity — let's keep developing the idea first. You said control gets 'split.' That actually connects to one of the prompt's core questions: is the State an *actor* that drives planning, or a *site* where different forces compete? What do you think, based on what you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now shifting from conceptual thinking to writing anxiety, which is a bit of a detour. My role is not to help them with language or writing — that's explicitly off-limits. I should redirect them back to deepening their conceptual understanding, since stronger ideas will naturally lead to clearer writing. I should also probe the 'split control' idea more, as it connects directly to the prompt's question about whether the state is a site or an actor.

exchanges.student: I think the state is both, ngl. It acts through laws/policy, but it’s also a place where donors, elites, agencies, and citizens all push for influence.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong and defensible position. Now think about what follows from that — if the state is both actor and site, how do you think collaboration and resistance emerge between these competing forces in multi-level planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a sophisticated and defensible position — the state as both actor and site. This is genuinely good thinking. I should affirm this briefly and then push them toward the next part of the prompt: how collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning. This keeps the conversation moving forward and ensures they're building toward a complete reflection.

exchanges.student: Maybe collaboration happens when their interests line up, and resistance happens when plans feel imposed or unequal? I’m not sure if that’s too basic tho.
exchanges.tutor: It's not too basic — but it is abstract. Can you think of a real-world example, from course readings or elsewhere, where a planning effort faced resistance because it felt imposed? What made it feel that way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is second-guessing a genuinely solid insight. I should briefly validate it without over-praising, and push them to make it more concrete by connecting it to a real example or context — this will strengthen their reflection and move away from abstraction. I should not help with writing.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on a reading rn tbh. Could I use a general example like donor-led urban redevelopment or decentralization reforms, or does it need one exact case?
exchanges.tutor: A general example works fine for a reflection. But just naming it isn't enough — what specifically about donor-led redevelopment or decentralization would illustrate your point about imposed planning and resistance? What's the mechanism you're pointing to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a logistical question about the assignment format. This is reasonable to address briefly. A general example is fine for a reflection piece, and I should affirm that while pushing them to actually develop the example rather than just name it — the specificity of their reasoning matters more than the source.

exchanges.student: I think the mechanism is that actors with money or formal authority set priorities from above, while local ppl have less say even though they’re affected most. Then resistance happens bc the plan lacks legitimacy, not just bc ppl dislike change.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong insight — distinguishing legitimacy-based resistance from simple change-aversion is exactly the kind of nuanced argument that makes a reflection compelling. You've now worked through most of the prompt's questions. What would a first attempt at a thesis or opening argument look like, pulling these threads together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a really significant conceptual leap here — distinguishing resistance rooted in lack of legitimacy from mere opposition to change. This directly and elegantly ties together the power, legitimacy, and resistance threads of the prompt. We've now covered most of the prompt's key questions through discussion. Given we're around exchange 8, I should signal that they have enough material to start writing and push them toward integration rather than continuing to explore concepts.

exchanges.student: Maybe: in multi-level planning, power comes from legal authority, resources, and legitimacy, so outcomes are shaped by uneven control across actors and scales. I think the state is both an actor and a site, which is why collaboration and resistance both happen depending on whose priorities get recognized.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid foundation — it hits the main threads of the prompt. One honest critique: the second sentence feels like two separate claims loosely joined. How do those two ideas — state as actor/site AND collaboration/resistance — actually connect causally? Tightening that link would strengthen your thesis. But overall, you have enough to start drafting. Give it a go!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis that integrates the key concepts we've discussed. It covers power sources, the state as actor/site, and the conditions for collaboration/resistance. This is good progress. Rather than evaluating it as a grade, I should give honest constructive feedback and push them to start writing the full reflection, since we've had a productive conceptual discussion and they have enough to work with.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I see that now — I think collaboration/resistance happen *because* the state is both actor and contested site, so different groups try to shape planning through it. I’m gonna draft from that, but if I get stuck I’ll ask abt structure.
exchanges.tutor: That's a much tighter causal argument — good. When you come back, bring what you've written. I can help you think through ideas, but structure and language are yours to figure out. Good luck drafting!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has sharpened the causal link in their thesis and is ready to draft. This is a good stopping point for our conceptual discussion. I should affirm their progress briefly, but also set a clear boundary about structure help — I can help them think through ideas but not help with writing or organization. I'll keep it short and encouraging.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. Key ideas were consistently student-generated: the student independently identified the three sources of power, the actor/site duality, and the legitimacy-based resistance mechanism. The tutor never gave away conclusions but instead asked progressively deeper questions that built on each prior student response. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, responses were concise and interactive, tone was supportive without being effusive, and the tutor explicitly declined to do structural/language work for the student at the end.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated key ideas before the student derived them. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the three-source insight and the actor/site claim before pushing further) and used diagnostic questioning rather than dumping information. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback in Turn 9, pointing out the loose causal link between two claims and explaining what tightening it would accomplish. Dialogue quality was strong: the conversation advanced steadily without repetition, stayed anchored to the prompt throughout, and no off-topic drift occurred. Communication was bite-sized and precise across all turns, tone was consistently warm and formative, and no grading language was used.
